• where the Bible actually comes from (not Europe)

• why denominations and interpretations multiplied
• how imagery shaped perception (white Jesus, European depictions)

• why control does NOT equal authorship

Who are the Israelites today?

In this episode, we examine Israelite identity through scripture, logic, and history — not European taxonomy, not modern labels, and not assumptions.

• how Israel is defined by covenant, seed, captivity, and inheritance

• how word studies are misused (etymological fallacy)
• why “ruddy” does not mean pale
• how Israelites appear in multiple regions, including African regions (Acts 13:1)
• why Herod being Idumean does not prove total population replacement
• how DNA arguments confuse geography with covenant identity

The question is not:

“What do people call themselves today?”

The question is:

Who fits the biblical pattern?

What is Babylon?

Is Babylon just an ancient empire that disappeared thousands of years ago…

or is Babylon something more?

In this episode of the Semitic Jew Podcast, we examine Babylon from a biblical, historical, and logical perspective — starting from Genesis, moving through Daniel, and into prophecy in Isaiah and Revelation.

We break down:

• the origin of Babylon at Babel under Nimrod
• the rise of Babylon as an empire under Nebuchadnezzar
• how Babylon operates as a system in prophecy
• and how patterns in scripture can be compared with historical and modern systems

This episode also addresses common errors in interpretation, including:

• limited choice fallacy
• anachronism
• appeal to popularity

The goal is simple:

Read the text distinctly.
Give the sense.
Cause understanding. — Nehemiah 8:8

#semiticjew #biblicalstudies #israeliteresearch Many critics claim the Bible supports slavery. But when people make that claim, are they actually describing what the Torah says — or are they projecting modern systems like the transatlantic slave trade onto an ancient legal text?

In this episode of the Semitic Jew Podcast, we examine what the scriptures actually say about servitude in the Torah and why the modern concept of racial chattel slavery is not the same as the servitude regulated in the laws of Moses.

We examine key passages from Exodus, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy, define the Hebrew term ʿeved, and discuss the biblical prohibition against kidnapping found in Exodus 21:16. We also explore how the New Testament condemns slave traders and emphasizes justice and brotherhood among believers.

Along the way we expose several logical fallacies often used in arguments against the Bible, including the strawman fallacy, false cause fallacy, appeal to emotion, and anachronism.

Topics covered in this episode:

• What the Hebrew word ʿeved actually means
• Why biblical servitude is not the same as modern chattel slavery
• The Torah’s laws about release, Jubilee, and economic restoration
• The prohibition of kidnapping and slave trading in scripture
• How critics project modern systems like plantation slavery onto ancient texts
• Logical fallacies commonly used in debates about the Bible and slavery

The goal is simple:

Read the text distinctly.
Give the sense.
Cause understanding.

#semiticjew #biblicalstudies #israeliteresearch In this episode of the Semitic Jew Podcast, we examine a question that has divided believers for centuries:

Did the New Testament cancel the Law of God?

Many claim that when Christ said He came to “fulfill the Law” in Matthew 5, it meant the Law was abolished. But does fulfillment actually mean cancellation? Or has that interpretation developed through theological tradition rather than the text itself?

In this episode we examine the words of Christ, the apostles, and the prophets through careful biblical reading and logical analysis.

We discuss:

• What Jesus meant by “fulfill the Law” in Matthew 5:17
• Whether fulfillment logically implies abolition
• Why Peter warned that Paul’s writings are often misunderstood
• What Paul actually says about the Law in Romans 3:31
• Why the apostles continued walking orderly and keeping the Law in Acts
• How Gentile kingdoms rule the earth until the Kingdom of God is established

Along the way we also examine common logical fallacies that appear in debates about the Law, including the strawman, red herring, and false cause fallacies.

The goal is simple:

To read the text distinctly, give the sense, and cause understanding.

Scripture.
Logic.
Truth.

Episode 4 Preview

Next episode we tackle another difficult topic:
Slavery in Leviticus — Text vs Modern Projection

Is it true that because ancient nations practiced slavery, the Bible therefore endorses slavery? Or are modern readers projecting modern assumptions onto ancient covenant law?
